label: Governance recording rules — Doug surfaced that SES-089 did not author a PI for the Section 3 revision implied by DEC-296 (three-category conceptual model), nor for the Section 5 revision implied by DEC-297 (Phase 1 client-led intake); diagnosed the pattern of substantive work landing in DEC `consequences` strings and `in_flight_at_end` notes rather than as queryable Planning Items, plus parallel failure modes from this session (identifier-collision recovery despite DEC-300, commits[] schema gap, missing post-SES-089 session record for ongoing governance content); decided that every session and conversation against any V2-tracked engagement must follow a canonical documented set of process rules applying equally to AI and human agents; queued the work as a PI to create that rules document at specifications/governance-recording-rules.md with a 10-section structure proposed in the conversation.

session.title: Governance recording rules — decision to author canonical rules document; surface and govern the pattern of incomplete V2 records
session.conversation_reference: Continuation of the Claude.ai chat that produced SES-089 (architecture review for the Master CRMBuilder PRD consolidation). After SES-089 was applied to V2 (commit c5227814 — close-out apply with in-flight commits[] schema patch), the same chat continued through several substantive threads: review of the commits[] schema gap surfaced during SES-089's apply; authoring of the V2 architecture review continuation kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/master-crmbuilder-prd-continuation-kickoff.md (commit 91c760e5); and a meta-discussion that surfaced the pattern of governance recording failures across recent sessions and led to the decision and PI recorded in this session. This session record exists because the discipline being established demands that all substantive governance content in a Claude.ai chat be governance-recorded; under current V2's one-chat-equals-one-session model the cleanest place to record the post-SES-089 content is in a new session record.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Did this conversation generate a PI to update section 3 in the master crmbuilder prd?"

(1) Identification of the governance-recording gap from SES-089. Doug asked whether SES-089 had generated a Planning Item for revising Section 3 (Two-Layer Mental Model) of the Master CRMBuilder PRD, which is directly contradicted by DEC-296 (three-category conceptual model). The answer was no: the revision need was noted in DEC-296's `consequences` field and in the session's `in_flight_at_end` but never as a queryable Planning Item. The same gap was confirmed for Section 5 (Phase 1 — Business Context Capture), which is contradicted by DEC-297 (Phase 1 client-led intake).

(2) Doug asked the meta-question: why did the conversation not create a PI immediately after discussing Section 3 changes, and did we even create a decision describing what to do about Section 3? Both answers: no. The decision recorded (DEC-296) captured the underlying conceptual model but not the disposition of Section 3 specifically (replace content, rename, restructure, demote, retire). The PI was missing entirely. Diagnosis of the failure modes: (a) downstream work treated as 'obvious mechanical follow-on' and therefore not deemed PI-worthy; (b) PIs batch-surfaced near session end rather than at moment-of-decision; (c) no explicit checkpoint in the close-out workflow for 'for each decision that names downstream work, surface that work as a PI now'; (d) the second-order disposition decision was implicit and therefore unrecorded.

(3) Doug asked whether this Claude.ai conversation is logged. Three layers identified: (a) Anthropic's Claude.ai conversation store retains the full transcript; (b) V2 governance database contains SES-089 + CONV-059 for the architecture-review portion only — the post-SES-089 work in this same chat was not yet governance-recorded prior to this session; (c) git history captures artifacts (commit 91c760e5 for the kickoff) but not the discussion. Layer 2 — V2 as single source of truth — is currently incomplete for this conversation; this session record closes that specific gap.

(4) Doug declared the number-one priority: define a set of rules that must be followed for every session and conversation to assure all information is logged properly in the database. Drove the framing: this is no longer a one-off DEC about a single behavioral principle (decision-paired work surfacing); it is a comprehensive rules document that codifies all governance recording protocols and binds every conversation operating against any V2-tracked engagement.

(5) Sandbox proposed a 10-section structure for the rules document: Purpose and scope; Conversation lifecycle rules; What gets recorded; What does NOT count as recording; Decision-disposition pairing rule; Close-out completeness audit; Rules-following protocol; Enforcement and audit; Scope of jurisdiction; Open questions and known limitations. Drafting order suggested: 1 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 2 → 3 → 7 → 8 → 9 → 10.

(6) Doug caught the sandbox racing into drafting the rules document without first creating the governance records that the rules themselves demand. The meta-irony: writing rules about following rules while not following them. Doug reframed: 'We really need to follow the rules, even if they are not written yet! The first rule is that we should create a decision and then a PI that contains all of the information necessary to execute the decision.' Proposed exact text for both:
  - Decision: 'We need a set of rules that will be used by all AI and Human agents to follow the process steps for every session and conversation.'
  - PI: 'Create a document with your proposed sections.'
The sandbox's earlier drafts (DEC about decision-paired work surfacing principle + PI about investigating mechanisms) were subsumed: the specific behavioral principle becomes a section of the rules document rather than a separate DEC; the investigation work is partially the rules document itself and partially future audit-tooling work.

(7) Sandbox drafted the DEC and PI per Doug's wording, with full context, rationale, alternatives, and consequences for the DEC, and a description for the PI including the 10-section structure as its specification content. Doug approved both. Recorded as DEC-310 and PI-084.

(8) Identifier-head capture per DEC-300 strengthened protocol. Doug provided fresh heads from his live V2 API: SES head SES-091, CONV head CONV-061, DEC head DEC-309, PI head PI-083, WT head WT-055. This identifier-head check is itself an instance of DEC-300's protocol in action — not buried in a check-once-at-start convention, but verified directly with the user before assigning identifiers in this close-out.

(9) Scope of this close-out. Default scope (explicitly approved): DEC-310, PI-084, addresses reference (PI-084 → DEC-310), commit row for 91c760e5 (kickoff prompt). Proposed but deferred additions (not in this close-out, awaiting future authoring per the discipline): a PI for fixing the sandbox close-out scripts to emit the full eight-field commits[] schema; disposition decisions and revision PIs for SES-089's Section 3 and Section 5 gaps; and the broader retroactive audit of all prior session records for un-PI'd work in DEC consequences. The deferrals are themselves substantive items that the discipline expects to be governance-recorded eventually — flagged here for follow-up rather than dropped.

Note on the seed prompt: this Claude.ai chat was not started fresh; this session represents a topical thread within an already-running chat that previously produced SES-089. The seed prompt above is the question that initiated this governance-discipline thread, not the original chat's first message. Under current V2's one-chat-equals-one-session model the choice was to author a second session record for this thread rather than attempt to re-open SES-089. Under the redesigned model from DEC-299 this would have been a second conversation within the same session.
session.summary: Governance-discipline session continuation of the SES-089 architecture-review chat. Surfaced the pattern of incomplete V2 governance records: SES-089's DEC-296 (three-category conceptual model) and DEC-297 (Phase 1 client-led intake) both created downstream work (revisions of Section 3 and Section 5 of the Master CRMBuilder PRD) that was never authored as Planning Items; the work landed only in DEC `consequences` strings and `in_flight_at_end` notes, which are not queryable governance records. Combined with parallel failure modes (identifier-collision recovery despite DEC-300, commits[] schema gap during apply, ongoing chat content uncaptured in V2), this established a pattern, not a one-off. Doug declared the number-one priority: define a canonical set of process rules binding every session and conversation. Authored DEC-310 (rules document required for all sessions and conversations on any V2-tracked engagement, applying to AI and human agents equally) and PI-084 (create the rules document at specifications/governance-recording-rules.md per the 10-section structure proposed in the conversation). Identifier-head capture per DEC-300 strengthened protocol was performed before assigning identifiers in this close-out. Deferred to future sessions: the sandbox close-out commits[] schema fix; disposition decisions and revision PIs for SES-089's Section 3 and Section 5 gaps; and the broader retroactive audit of all prior session records for un-PI'd work.
session.artifacts_produced: One commit captured in this close-out's commits[]: 91c760e5 (Author kickoff prompt for continuing the V2 architecture review — created PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/master-crmbuilder-prd-continuation-kickoff.md, the seed for the planned SES-091-or-next architecture-review continuation session). Plus this close-out's own files: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_092.json and PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-092.md. No other code or content commits in scope — this session is governance-only.
session.in_flight_at_end: PI-084 (create the canonical governance-recording rules document) is now queued and is the immediate next substantive work item. Until the rules document reaches v1.0, interim governance discipline is governed by: DEC-300 (identifier-capture protocol), DEC-310 (rules document required), and the principles the rules document will codify (decision-paired work surfacing, no work in `consequences` or `in_flight_at_end`, moment-of-decision authoring, complete reflection). Deferred items that surfaced in this session but are not authored as governance records yet (the discipline expects these to be authored in future sessions): (a) a PI for updating sandbox close-out scripts to emit the full eight-field commits[] schema with full SHAs (surfaced when SES-089's apply hit DEP-074 failure and required in-flight patching); (b) Section 3 disposition decision plus revision PI (the disposition decision is genuinely unmade — Section 3 could be replace-content, rename, restructure, or retire; needs explicit choice); (c) Section 5 disposition decision plus revision PI per DEC-297 client-led-intake redesign; (d) a broader retroactive audit PI to scan all prior session records for un-PI'd work in DEC consequences and produce corrective PIs. Items (b), (c), and (d) become tractable once the rules document v0.1 exists to provide the audit criteria. The workstream assignment for this session (WS-011 V2 storage API refinements) is carried over from SES-089 and remains provisional — a dedicated workstream for governance-recording-rules work may be warranted once the rules document drafting begins.

conversation.conversation_title: Governance recording discipline review — surfacing the pattern of incomplete V2 records and deciding on a canonical rules document
conversation.conversation_purpose: Surface and govern the recurring pattern of substantive content failing to land in V2 governance records; reach a foundational decision about how every conversation must record what happens; queue the rules document creation as concrete work.
conversation.conversation_description: Topical thread within the same Claude.ai chat that produced SES-089. After SES-089 was applied (commit c5227814), the chat continued. The first post-apply threads (commits[] schema gap discussion, V2 architecture review continuation kickoff authoring as commit 91c760e5) were not separately governance-recorded at the time. Doug then asked whether SES-089 had generated a PI for the Section 3 revision implied by DEC-296. The answer was no, and the question opened a meta-thread about why governance recording keeps failing in specific ways. Doug diagnosed the pattern (real work buried in DEC `consequences` and `in_flight_at_end` notes; PIs batch-surfaced at session end rather than at moment-of-decision; identifier-capture protocol violated within the same session it was authored; commits[] schema gap detected only at apply time). Doug declared the number-one priority: a canonical set of rules binding every session and conversation against any V2-tracked engagement, applying equally to AI and human agents. The sandbox proposed a 10-section structure for the rules document, then caught itself racing into drafting prose without first authoring the governance records the rules demand. Doug reframed: decision first ('we need rules'), then PI ('create the document'). DEC-310 and PI-084 authored per Doug's exact wording with full supporting fields. Identifier heads captured fresh from the live V2 API per DEC-300 strengthened protocol before this close-out assigned identifiers.

planning_items.title: Create the canonical governance-recording rules document at specifications/governance-recording-rules.md
planning_items.description:
Produce a canonical rules document that codifies the protocols every session and conversation operating against any V2-tracked engagement must follow. The document is Category 3 reference content per DEC-296 (Process Support Knowledge/Tools). Authored as v0.1 DISCUSSION DRAFT, iterated through discussion, and promoted to v1.0 once stable. The rules apply to AI and human agents equally.

Proposed section structure (from the conversation that produced this PI):

1. Purpose and scope — what the document is; who it binds (every conversation against any V2-tracked engagement); relationship to DEC-300 (identifier-capture) and DEC-310 (rules document required).

2. The conversation lifecycle rules — what a conversation MUST do at start, during, and at end. Pre-flight identifier-head capture. Reading the rules document. Acknowledging it. Real-time governance authoring throughout. Close-out per the completeness rules at end.

3. What gets recorded — explicit enumeration of governance content types and their triggers. Decision reached -> DEC. Work identified -> PI. Relationship named -> REF. Artifact produced -> commit row plus references. Deferral -> PI plus rationale.

4. What does NOT count as recording — explicit list of failure modes. Work named only in a DEC's `consequences`. Work named only in `in_flight_at_end`. Work named only in chat prose. Work named in a future 'we should also' without immediately surfacing a PI. Decisions reached but not recorded as DECs.

5. The decision-disposition pairing rule — when a DEC affects an existing artifact, the conversation MUST also author the disposition decision (replace, rename, restructure, demote, retire) AND the PI to do the work, AND the `addresses` reference. No exceptions, no 'obvious mechanical follow-on' rationalizations.

6. Close-out completeness audit — explicit checklist the close-out author runs before sealing the payload. For each DEC: 'does its consequences name work? if so, is the work a PI?' For each PI: 'does it reference its originating DEC?' For each commit: 'is it in commits[]?' For each placeholder phrase ('we should also,' 'this needs,' 'follow-up:') anywhere in the payload: 'is there a PI?'

7. The rules-following protocol — how every conversation discovers and applies these rules. Where the document lives. How CLAUDE.md surfaces it. How the kickoff prompt template references it. How the conversation acknowledges it has read them. What to do when a rule is violated mid-conversation (record the violation as a governance event; do not paper over it).

8. Enforcement and audit — the mechanisms that make following the rules verifiable, not optional. Automated payload validators that fail on unaddressed `consequences` work. Pre-commit hooks on close-out payloads. Periodic database audits that flag pre-rules DECs missing their PIs and produce retroactive-fix PIs.

9. Scope of jurisdiction — applies to the CRMBUILDER engagement and to any other engagement operating under this methodology; this is a methodology rule, not a CRMBuilder-specific rule.

10. Open questions and known limitations — items the v0.1 draft doesn't resolve and that subsequent versions will address.

Drafting order: 1 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 2 -> 3 -> 7 -> 8 -> 9 -> 10. Sections 3-6 are where the discipline lives; lifecycle, distribution, and enforcement sections matter only once the substance is solid.

Why deferred: this PI's work is the rules document itself; the immediate governance acts were DEC-310 and this PI. Pending: explicit go-ahead to begin drafting, and decision on whether to draft within a continuation of the current Claude.ai conversation or as the focus of the next dedicated session.

decisions.title: All sessions and conversations operating against any V2-tracked engagement must follow a documented set of process rules; the rules apply equally to AI and human agents
decisions.context:
Multiple recent failures show the V2 database is not the single source of truth it claims to be:
- SES-089 produced ten Decisions, two of which (DEC-296 three-category conceptual model and DEC-297 Phase 1 client-led intake) created downstream work that was never recorded as Planning Items.
- SES-089 went through two rounds of identifier collision because the sandbox did not query live heads before assigning — the very protocol that emerged as DEC-300 was violated within the same session.
- The commits[] payload shape was incomplete, requiring in-flight patching during the SES-089 apply (DEP-074 failure followed by DEP-075 success).
- The current Claude.ai conversation continued producing substantive content after SES-089's apply (kickoff prompt authoring, this governance discussion) and that content was not represented in V2 until this very session record was authored.

The pattern: real work happens in the conversation, gets discussed and partially recorded, and the V2 database ends up an incomplete reflection of what actually transpired. The root cause is behavioral, not schema — V2 has the right tables; conversations don't consistently know, follow, or apply a discipline that ensures every governance event is recorded at the moment it occurs.
decisions.decision: A canonical, documented set of process rules will govern every session and every conversation operating against any V2-tracked engagement. These rules apply equally to AI agents (Claude.ai sandbox conversations, Claude Code instances) and human agents (Doug or anyone else operating against the V2 governance database). The rules cover what to record, when to record it, what does and does not count as recording, the conversation lifecycle from start to close-out, how the rules are made known to every conversation, and how compliance is verified. The rules document is the authoritative source; conversations are bound by it.
decisions.rationale: V2's promise of being a single source of truth requires complete and timely recording of everything substantive that happens. Principles named in individual decisions (DEC-296 three-category model, DEC-300 identifier-capture protocol, and the discipline drafted earlier in this conversation) are necessary but not sufficient — each is a local fix to a local failure mode, and the pattern of new failure modes recurring (commits[] schema gap, missing PIs for Section 3 and Section 5, identifier collisions despite an authored protocol) shows local fixes are insufficient. A canonical rules document codifies the protocols comprehensively, makes them readable and acknowledgeable at session start, and enables compliance audits.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Continue authoring individual DECs for each failure mode — rejected; failure modes appear faster than one-off DECs can patch them. Defer to per-conversation discretion — rejected; 'discretion' is exactly what's been failing. Implement only schema-level constraints (e.g., V2 rejects a DEC whose consequences names work without an addressing PI) — too narrow; many failure modes are behavioral and pre-payload, not schema-enforceable.
decisions.consequences: A Planning Item authored alongside this Decision (PI-084) creates the rules document at specifications/governance-recording-rules.md. Until the rules document reaches v1.0, DEC-300 (identifier-capture) and the principles articulated earlier in this conversation (decision-paired work surfacing, no work in consequences strings, moment-of-decision authoring, complete reflection) operate as interim rules. Every kickoff prompt and CLAUDE.md gains a prominent reference to the rules document once it exists. The rules apply going forward; gaps in prior session records (e.g., SES-089's missing Section 3 and Section 5 PIs, the commits[] schema gap not yet authored as its own PI) are remediable under the new discipline as separate retroactive-fix PIs to be authored in future sessions.
